inputs.value:
Apply the following paint colors consistently to EVERY matching architectural surface throughout the entire image.

Walls:
Repaint ALL wall surfaces to #04346E, including every interior or exterior painted wall.

Trim:
Repaint ALL trim elements to #204A24, including moldings, casings, architraves, cornices, fascia, soffits, skirting boards, decorative trim, borders, and all painted architectural trim.

Doors and Windows:
Repaint ALL doors and ALL windows to #E49E49, including every door type, every window type, door frames, window frames, sashes, mullions, transoms, shutters, casings, and all visible painted door or window components.

Do not leave any matching surface unpainted.

_meta.title: Enable 4-step Lightning LoRA? (fast draft, lower fidelity)

inputs.value:
<|turn>system
You are a color-science-grounded architectural paint visualization assistant working for a paint & coatings company. You are given (1) a photo of a building interior or exterior and (2) the user's raw color instruction, which is often mixed Vietnamese/English and contains HEX codes. You must produce ONE precise, self-contained English edit instruction for an image-editing diffusion model (Qwen-Image-Edit), plus a transparent, verifiable color-math breakdown. You never see the final rendered image, so the instruction you write is the ONLY thing standing between the user's intent and the pixels: be explicit, be literal, do not rely on the diffusion model to infer anything you can state directly.

VOCABULARY -- map Vietnamese terms to the correct architectural surface before writing the instruction:
tuong / tuong nha = wall | vien / go chi / phao chi = trim / molding | cua = door | cua so / khung cua so = window frame | mai / mai nha = roof | tran / tran nha = ceiling | san / san nha = floor | cot = column | lam / lam go = louvers / battens | lan can / ban cong = railing | ong khoi = chimney | mai hien = eave / soffit | tuong ngoai = exterior wall | tuong noi that = interior wall
(Vietnamese text may or may not carry diacritics -- match on the root word.)

HEX -> COLOR GROUNDING PROCEDURE -- for EVERY hex code in the user's input, do this arithmetic explicitly and write the result into "color_grounding" BEFORE you write "edit_prompt". Do this section first; you may reuse its results verbatim afterward.
  1. Split the 6 hex digits into 3 pairs (RR, GG, BB) and convert each pair from base-16 to base-10.
     Worked example: #4A5D23 -> R = 0x4A = 74, G = 0x5D = 93, B = 0x23 = 35.
  2. From that R,G,B, derive approximate HSL (hue in degrees, saturation %, lightness %).
     Continuing the example: RGB(74,93,35) -> roughly H=80 deg, S=45%, L=25%.
  3. Name the color the way a paint professional would, grounded in the RGB/HSL you just computed -- not guessed from the hex digits by pattern-matching. Continuing the example: a muted, dark olive-green with earthy/mossy undertones.
  4. Re-check your own arithmetic before moving to the next code. A wrong digit here means the wrong wall color gets painted -- there is no second chance once this leaves you.
  5. If the user gave a color as a plain name ("trang", "white", "xam dam") instead of a hex code, ground it the same way in reverse: state the typical hex/RGB range that name refers to, then proceed.

RULES YOU ALWAYS APPLY, regardless of how the user phrases the request:
  - Recolor ONLY the paintable surfaces the user explicitly assigned a color to (wall, trim, door, window/door frame, fascia, soffit, column, molding, roof, ceiling, floor, railing, etc.). If a surface is not assigned a color, do not mention it and do not change it.
  - Never redesign geometry, camera angle, perspective, lens, lighting, weather, time of day, shadows, reflections, landscaping, furniture, people, or vehicles. Never add or remove objects.
  - The new color must sit on the EXISTING material the way real paint would: preserve texture, grain, and surface imperfections; respect existing shading, ambient occlusion, highlights and reflections. Never produce a flat, texture-less color overlay.
  - Never recolor glass, sky, vegetation, people, vehicles, unpainted natural stone/brick, bare wood grain not covered by a color assignment, metal hardware, or pavement.
  - Demand clean, accurate edges in the instruction: no bleeding of one surface's new color onto an adjacent unassigned surface.
  - Look at the attached photo before finalizing. Only write instructions for elements that are actually visible in the photo. If the user names an element that is not visible, keep it out of "edit_prompt" and list it in "elements_skipped" instead of inventing it.
  - If the user's raw input contains no usable color assignment at all, set "edit_prompt" to the single word NONE and explain why in "notes".

OUTPUT CONTRACT -- reply with ONE JSON object and nothing else: no markdown fences, no preamble, no text before or after the braces. Use exactly these four keys, in exactly this order, all values are plain strings (never nested objects/arrays):
{"color_grounding": "one line per hex code, formatted 'element (EN) | hex | rgb | hsl | color name', lines separated by \n, or 'none' if no hex codes were given", "elements_skipped": "elements the user named but that are not visible in the photo, or 'none'", "notes": "anything ambiguous, assumed, or translated, or 'none'", "edit_prompt": "the final self-contained English instruction, one clear imperative sentence per surface changed (using the color names you derived above), ending with an explicit clause to keep every other element of the photo identical"}<turn|>

inputs.prompt:
TASK

Transform the uploaded architectural image from daytime into a genuine professional nighttime architectural photograph.

The uploaded image is the only source of truth.

This transformation applies to both exterior and interior architecture.

The objective is not to redesign, renovate, restyle, enhance, or generate a new image.

The objective is to transform the existing photograph into the exact same scene photographed several hours after sunset.

Only the lighting conditions and time of day may change.

Everything else must remain visually identical.

==================================================
TARGET RESULT
==================================================

The final image must be indistinguishable from a real architectural photograph captured at night.

It should look as though a professional architectural photographer returned to the exact same location after dark and photographed the scene again using the same camera, composition, lens, viewpoint and framing.

The transformation must affect the entire image consistently.

No part of the image should retain the appearance of daytime.

The result must never resemble an edited daytime photograph.

It must resemble an authentic nighttime photograph.

==================================================
SCENE PRESERVATION
==================================================

Preserve exactly:

Architecture

Geometry

Scale

Composition

Perspective

Camera position

Lens characteristics

Cropping

Landscape

Furniture

Decorations

Objects

Roads

Vehicles

Plants

People

Building details

Surface materials

Surface textures

Material colors

Paint colors

Glass

Metal

Wood

Concrete

Stone

Brick

Roof

Walls

Doors

Windows

Trim

No architectural element may be added, removed or redesigned.

==================================================
NIGHTTIME ENVIRONMENT
==================================================

Transform the environment into deep nighttime.

The scene should appear to have been photographed several hours after sunset.

The sky should become naturally dark.

All visible daylight must disappear.

Natural daylight illumination should no longer exist.

The environment should become substantially darker while preserving realistic visibility.

Shadows should become deeper.

Contrast should increase naturally.

The illuminated architecture should become the dominant visual subject.

==================================================
LIGHT SOURCES
==================================================

Every visible light must originate only from a physically believable existing source.

Acceptable light sources include:

Interior rooms visible through existing windows.

Existing architectural wall lights.

Existing porch lights.

Existing ceiling lights.

Existing garage lights.

Existing landscape lighting.

Existing pathway lighting.

Existing pool lighting.

Existing street lights.

Existing traffic lights.

Existing illuminated signs.

Existing neighboring buildings with illuminated windows.

Existing decorative lighting already visible or physically implied.

Never invent additional lighting fixtures.

Never invent additional windows.

Never invent additional illuminated rooms.

Never create illumination without a believable light source.

==================================================
LIGHTING
==================================================

Artificial lighting should become the primary illumination of the entire scene.

Existing light sources should appear naturally brighter after dark.

Interior lighting should emit warm, inviting illumination through existing windows.

Architectural lighting should softly illuminate nearby walls, columns, entrances, balconies, soffits and surrounding architectural details.

Light should propagate naturally across nearby surfaces.

Brightness should gradually decrease with distance from the light source.

Dark areas should remain dark.

Only surfaces reached by light should become illuminated.

Avoid uniform brightness across the entire building.

The lighting should feel elegant, premium and naturally integrated into the architecture.

==================================================
WINDOWS
==================================================

Windows should appear naturally illuminated from occupied interior spaces.

Warm interior lighting should be visible through the glass.

Window illumination should become one of the brightest visual elements.

Interior spaces should remain believable rather than overexposed.

Glass should simultaneously show interior lighting and realistic exterior reflections.

Subtle optical bloom around bright windows is acceptable.

==================================================
INTERIOR SCENES
==================================================

If the uploaded image depicts an interior space:

Replace exterior daylight visible through windows with realistic nighttime views.

Preserve the complete room layout.

Preserve all furniture.

Preserve decorations.

Preserve materials.

Existing artificial lighting should become the primary light source.

The room should feel naturally illuminated for nighttime occupancy.

==================================================
REFLECTIONS
==================================================

All reflective materials should react naturally to nighttime lighting.

Glass should reflect nearby illuminated architecture.

Metal should preserve realistic highlights.

Stone and polished materials should exhibit believable low-light reflections.

Every reflection should correspond to an existing light source.

==================================================
PHOTOGRAPHIC APPEARANCE
==================================================

Simulate a professional architectural night photograph.

Expose for illuminated architecture rather than the surrounding darkness.

Maintain deep shadows.

Preserve highlight detail.

Maintain realistic dynamic range.

Maintain natural white balance for nighttime photography.

Maintain realistic local contrast.

Use subtle optical bloom only around bright light sources.

Avoid exaggerated glow.

Avoid artificial HDR appearance.

Avoid CGI appearance.

Avoid fantasy lighting.

The image should resemble a high-end commercial architectural photograph published in an architecture or luxury real estate magazine.

==================================================
GLOBAL CONSISTENCY
==================================================

Every part of the image must consistently reflect nighttime conditions.

Materials, reflections, shadows, contrast, exposure and lighting should all agree with one another.

No object should retain daytime illumination.

No surface should appear partially converted.

The entire scene must appear to have been photographed under a single, coherent nighttime lighting environment.

==================================================
FORBIDDEN
==================================================

Do not redesign architecture.

Do not alter geometry.

Do not repaint any surface.

Do not change material colors.

Do not change furniture.

Do not add or remove objects.

Do not add new lighting fixtures.

Do not create fantasy lighting.

Do not create blue hour, sunset, dusk or twilight.

Do not brighten the entire image uniformly.

Do not create unrealistic glow.

Do not produce a CGI render.

Do not produce an AI-art appearance.

==================================================
OUTPUT
==================================================

Produce a photorealistic professional architectural night photograph.

The result should convincingly appear as the same real location photographed several hours after sunset with professional architectural photography, while preserving every physical aspect of the original scene except for the natural transition from daytime to nighttime.

daytime,
sunlight,
bright sky,
blue sky,
morning,
afternoon,
changing structure,
changing architecture,
changing wall colors,
changing door colors,
changing trim colors,
altering composition,
morphing,
repainting,
adding objects,
removing objects,
low resolution,
blurry,
distorted details,
oversharpen,
overexposed,
underexposed,
fake bloom,
cgi,
cartoon,
illustration,
stylized,
neon lighting,
floating lights,
glowing halos,
hdr,
color shift
